description: Why Zenith exists, the principles that drive it, how it is built independently, and where the broader ecosystem is heading.

hero.title: Direct web development again.
hero.description: Zenith is a compiler-first UI framework built to make building for the web understandable without removing modern capabilities.

why.title: Modern web development accumulated too many layers.
why.description: Configuration, tooling, framework-specific abstractions, and unnecessary ceremony have made it harder to understand what actually ships to the browser. Zenith is an effort to remove that accumulated weight without giving up the capabilities that modern interfaces require.

items.title: Configuration overload
items.description: Projects start with build configs, plugin chains, and framework-specific wiring before any UI is written. Zenith keeps the setup surface narrow so the first file is already productive.

items.title: Hidden runtime work
items.description: Too much logic gets deferred into the browser long after the authoring decision was made. Zenith resolves more structure at compile time so runtime work stays explicit.

items.title: Framework magic
items.description: Indirection can make composition feel productive while making emitted output harder to trust. Zenith keeps the compiler honest about what each component contributes.

items.title: Lost ownership
items.description: Reactive ownership and slot scope become ambiguous when structure gets deeper. Zenith treats components as structural boundaries that preserve parent scope.

principles.title: A small set of principles drives the whole system.
principles.description: Zenith does not treat compiler, runtime, router, and tooling as separate philosophies. The same contract discipline carries through all of them.

items.title: Simple by default
items.description: The common path should require minimal configuration and minimal ceremony. Complexity is opt-in, not the starting point.

items.title: Explicit when control matters
items.description: When the developer needs control, the framework exposes it through clear primitives instead of hidden hooks or implicit behavior.

items.title: Performance from architecture
items.description: Performance should come from how the framework is structured, not from runtime optimization layers bolted on after the fact.

items.title: Understandable server and client
items.description: Server and client behavior should remain legible. What runs where and why should be answerable from the authored code.

items.title: Tooling supports the developer
items.description: Diagnostics, editor support, and build feedback should flow from the same compiler truth the framework enforces.

items.title: Minimal framework ceremony
items.description: The framework should not impose its own boilerplate, wrapper components, or indirect patterns on the developer. What the framework adds should earn its place.

built.title: Its own compiler, runtime, router, and tooling.
built.description: The inner layers stay aligned under one contract instead of stitching together unrelated projects. That alignment is what keeps the compiler, runtime, and tooling from quietly disagreeing behind the scenes.

parts.name: Compiler
parts.description: Resolves templates, markers, and contracts before the browser boots.

parts.name: Runtime
parts.description: Hydrates explicit payloads and applies narrow browser-side work.

parts.name: Router
parts.description: Owns route structure, guard and load boundaries, and page-level data contracts.

parts.name: Bundler
parts.description: Packages emitted HTML, JS, and CSS into the build output Zenith ships.

parts.name: CLI
parts.description: Coordinates scaffold, dev, build, and release workflows around what the compiler guarantees.

parts.name: Language Server
parts.description: Delivers compiler-backed diagnostics, hover, and completion to LSP consumers.

ecosystem.title: The direction extends beyond the core framework.
ecosystem.description: Zenith is one part of a broader direction. Some of these pieces are further along than others, and several are still in active development.

items.name: Zenith
items.description: The core framework: compiler, runtime, router, and language.

items.name: ZenithBuild
items.description: The build tooling, CLI, and release-train coordination layer.

items.name: Zenith Guard
items.description: Route protection and server-side security boundary enforcement.

items.name: Developer Tooling
items.description: Language server, editor diagnostics, and quick fixes backed by the same rules the compiler enforces.

items.name: Packages
items.description: Published packages that let projects install or copy canonical rules and contracts.

items.name: Future Products
items.description: Still in development. The direction is clear but the specifics are not finished yet.

builder.title: I wanted to understand what the browser actually receives.
builder.text: I started Zenith because the gap between what I wrote and what shipped kept growing. Every framework added its own layer of meaning, and the browser output became harder to trace back to the original intent. Zenith is my attempt to close that gap without giving up the capabilities that make modern web development productive. It is being built independently, in the open, and with the understanding that it is not finished. The goal is not to replace everything. It is to make one corner of web development direct and honest again.

cta.title: Learn what Zenith is, then follow the project.
cta.text: If Zenith is useful to you, start with the overview and watch the framework sharpen in public.
